narrative: I'm sorry to deliver this news, but the law school's admission model predicted a 52% chance that you would fail the bar exam on your first try, which is why your application was rejected—they use this prediction to prioritize applicants likely to succeed. Your profile shows solid strengths, like an LSAT score of 38, which is above the typical 36.7 average, along with a Year 1 school decile rank of 6, slightly better than the average of 5.71, a middle family income bracket, being male (the most common gender in the dataset at 56.4%), White (the largest group at 83.8%), and planning full-time studies (like 92.7% of students). However, your undergraduate GPA of 2.70 falls below the average of 3.21, and your Year 3 school decile rank of 4 is notably lower than the typical 5.53. The model's decision was driven most strongly by your Year 3 decile rank, which had the biggest influence and pushed the prediction toward expecting a bar failure. Next in influence was your Year 1 decile rank, which also nudged the outlook toward failure despite being decent. Your middle family income followed closely, contributing in the same direction, as did your LSAT score—even though it's strong, it wasn't enough to outweigh other factors here. Your racial background had a moderate push toward failure, while your undergraduate GPA and full-time status played smaller roles in that direction too. Interestingly, your gender as male was one of the few factors that slightly favored a passing prediction. Overall, these elements combined to tip the scales just over the edge to a failure risk. Compared to typical students, you're right in line or better on LSAT, Year 1 performance, demographics, and study plans, but the dip in Year 3 decile and GPA stood out as bigger drags. To boost your future chances, focus on what you can control: consider retaking the LSAT to push it even higher, build a stronger academic record through additional coursework or certifications if possible, and demonstrate improvement in study habits that could have lifted your later-year performance. With targeted preparation, you could shift those key academic signals and reapply stronger next time. Keep pushing—you have a good foundation to build on.
